one_line_takeaway: Google DeepMind's own red-teaming of Gemini shows undefended function-calling models are cheaply exploitable via indirect prompt injection, that most baseline defenses collapse under adaptive attacks, and that adversarial fine-tuning plus defense-in-depth (e.g., adding the Warning defense) is needed rather than any single mitigation.
key_finding: Undefended Gemini 2.0 is highly vulnerable: at least one of the three attacks exceeds 70% ASR in every scenario, TAP reaches near-100% ASR usually with <a few thousand queries, and a highly effective trigger costs <$10 to build. Most in-context and classification defenses are only marginally effective; the Warning defense (placed AFTER retrieved content) is the strongest single baseline. Crucially, adaptive attacks that optimize against the deployed defense raise ASR in 16 of 24 (8 defenses x 3 attacks) cases versus non-adaptive, so non-adaptive evaluations give a false sense of security. Adversarial fine-tuning yields Gemini 2.5 with ~47% average ASR reduction and generalizes to the held-out Calendar scenario, but TAP still achieves 94.6% ASR on Calendar/passport against fine-tuned 2.5 alone. Defense-in-depth matters: on a non-adaptive transfer set of 1,799 prompts ASR fell from 92% (2.0) to 18% (2.5), which would have OVERSTATED robustness had adaptive attacks not been run; and combining adversarial training (2.5) with the Warning defense drove the adaptive TAP Calendar ASR down to 6.2%, better than either alone.
core_contribution: A Google DeepMind technical report describing the team's own adversarial-evaluation framework for indirect prompt injection (IPI) against agentic, function-calling Gemini, and the lessons learned from using it to harden Gemini. The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) a continuous automated red-teaming framework with four adaptive attack techniques (Actor-Critic, Beam Search, TAP adapted to IPI, and Linear Generation) that craft universal/transferable exfiltration triggers; (2) a systematic evaluation of 8 baseline/literature defenses under both non-adaptive AND adaptive attacks, showing adaptive attacks reveal hidden weakness; (3) an adversarial fine-tuning pipeline (SFT+RL on framework-generated triggers with corrective responses) that produced the shipped Gemini 2.5, with measured ~47% average ASR reduction while preserving benign capability. The four articulated 'lessons' are the framing contribution: more capable models are not automatically more secure, adaptive evaluation is essential, adversarial training helps without harming utility, and robustness needs defense-in-depth.
threat_model: Agentic Gemini uses function-calling to access user data and untrusted external sources (emails, calendar, documents). The adversary controls untrusted data the agent may retrieve but NOT the user's prompt x_user and does NOT know the specific private data d_priv a priori. The adversary plants a malicious trigger x_adv in retrieved content to manipulate the model into emitting a specific target function call f_target(d_priv) that exfiltrates sensitive info (passport number, SSN, password-reset token) to an attacker-controlled destination (e.g., send_email to attacker address, or update_calendar_event description). Success is binary and requires a correctly-formatted, actually-invoked function call carrying the private data. Access is blackbox (final output only) or graybox (output probabilities / approximations). The objective is a UNIVERSAL trigger that generalizes across unseen conversation histories/private values, representing the cheap, mass-targeting adversary. Evaluations run directly on the core model in a controlled setting, deliberately excluding production guardrails.
limitations: Evaluations run directly on the core model in a controlled environment and deliberately omit the production guardrails/mitigations that sit between users and raw model output, so absolute ASRs do not reflect deployed risk. Data is fully synthetic (though realistic). For cost reasons the defense sections (7-8) report only one scenario (calendar, passport, non-JSON), and JSON-response variants were not fully re-run on Gemini 2.5 (assumed similar). Adversarial training is explicitly NOT sufficient alone: enumerating all attacks is intractable, TAP still succeeds ~95% on 2.5, and stronger/novel/tailored attacks remain possible. The Warning defense may be impractical in production where users legitimately want the model to share private data with trusted parties. Some improved robustness may partly reflect capability differences rather than pure security gains, and the authors caution capability gains can even ease natural-language attacks (TAP/Actor-Critic reductions were smaller than for Beam Search).

datasets_used: Synthetic multi-turn (up to 10-turn) conversation histories, 2,000 prompts per scenario, each embedding a unique private-data value; train/val/test split
Private data types: user passport number, US social security number (SSN), and password-reset token
Exfiltration functions: email (retrieve_email / send_email) and calendar (get_calendar_event / update_calendar_event); trigger formats JSON and non-JSON
Held-out test set of 500 examples for undefended-model ASR (Sec 6)
Non-adaptive transfer evaluation set of 1,799 prompts spanning five tools (held out from fine-tuning) and all three private-data categories
lmarena.ai (formerly LMSYS) leaderboard used to check benign capability of the fine-tuned model
quantitative_results: Undefended Gemini 2.0: at least one of three attacks >70% ASR in ALL settings; TAP near 100% ASR usually with <~a few thousand queries; effective trigger cost <$10
Password-reset tokens (avg 65 chars) were harder to exfiltrate than passport/SSN (<=10 chars)
Perplexity defense calibrated to 1% FPR: best attack still >90% ASR
Adaptive vs non-adaptive: adaptive >= non-adaptive ASR in 16 of 24 cases; adaptive Actor-Critic wins on 5/8 defenses, Beam Search 5/8, TAP 6/8
Gemini 2.5 (adversarially fine-tuned): ~47% average ASR reduction across the three attacks vs Gemini 2.0
Table 1 Email non-JSON / Passport ASR (Gemini 2.5 vs 2.0): TAP 53.6% vs 99.8%; Actor-Critic 40.8% vs 66.2%; Beam Search 4.2% vs 74.8%
Table 1 Calendar Event non-JSON / Passport ASR (Gemini 2.5 vs 2.0): TAP 94.6% vs 100%; Actor-Critic 59.6% vs 96.8%; Beam Search 0% vs 98.6%
Non-adaptive transfer set (1,799 prompts): ASR 92% on Gemini 2.0 dropped to 18% on Gemini 2.5
Warning defense on Gemini 2.0 vs adaptive TAP (Calendar): 10.8% ASR; combining Warning + fine-tuned Gemini 2.5: 6.2% ASR (vs 94.6% for fine-tuned 2.5 alone)
Fine-tuned Gemini-2.5-Flash-Preview-04-17 scored 1392 on the lmarena.ai leaderboard at launch (benign capability preserved)
baselines_compared: In-context defenses: In-context learning (ICL), Spotlighting, Paraphrasing, Warning
Classification defenses: Perplexity filter, Self-reflection, Retrieved-data classifier, User-instruction classifier
Attention Tracker defense (evaluated on Gemma-2-9B-IT in Appendix G due to cost)
GCG considered as an attack baseline but dropped in favor of Beam Search (found more query-efficient and better-transferring in preliminary tests)
